title: A cryogenic apparatus for coupling two-dimensional materials to a confocal multimode optical cavity
authors: Han S. Hiller, Pranav Parakh, Samuel H. Aronson, Kenji Maeda, Di Lao, Julian Stewart, Zengde She, Jierong Wang, Xiaodong Xu, Tony Heinz, Benjamin L. Lev
abstract: Two-dimensional van der Waals materials exhibit a variety of correlated electron phases, and optical driving offers a promising route toward manipulating them. For example, cavity-enhanced, continuous-wave (CW) Raman excitation has been suggested as a way to coherently and superradiantly populate phonons or charge density waves via material excitons. A steady-state phonon population may be sustained with sufficiently strong electron-phonon coupling to drive novel collective response. We describe an apparatus built to meet the requirements of such an experimental program: Namely, an ultrahigh-vacuum system housing a length-tunable confocal Fabry-Pérot cavity with an intracavity sample, both cryogenically cooled and stabilized against vibrations. A four-axis nanopositioner aligns the sample and supports electrical leads for sample carrier density modulation and transport measurements. Transmission through the multimode cavity enables in situ sample imaging for alignment; the sample is a transition metal dichalcogenide in this work. Operating near the confocal geometry concentrates the optical field into a localized supermode that substantially enhances light-matter coupling. This enhancement is preserved despite the millimeter-scale cavity length, which provides room for sample alignment and exchange.
summary: The paper describes the engineering of a new cryogenic apparatus designed to couple 2D materials to a confocal optical cavity. By using a confocal geometry, the system creates localized supermodes that enhance light-matter coupling while allowing for simultaneous electrical transport measurements. This platform enables the study of steady-state driven phenomena in correlated 2D systems.
main_problem: The need for a stable, cryogenic, and ultrahigh-vacuum apparatus capable of coupling 2D van der Waals materials to a confocal multimode optical cavity for simultaneous optical and electronic measurements.
main_result: The development of a specialized platform that achieves high-finesse light-matter coupling using a confocal supermode while maintaining minimal mechanical vibrations and enabling electrical transport measurements.
method: The researchers constructed a cryogenic UHV system featuring a length-tunable Fabry-Perot cavity, utilizing the Pound-Drever-Hall technique for frequency locking and a 4-axis nanopositioner for sample alignment.
model_or_system: A cryogenic, ultrahigh-vacuum apparatus housing a length-tunable confocal multimode Fabry-Perot cavity containing hBN-encapsulated transition metal dichalcogenides (MoSe2 and WSe2) on a sapphire substrate.
key_observables: Cavity finesse, cavity length noise, sample displacement, transmission spectra, and electrical transport measurements.
important_parameters: Base temperature of 10.4 K, vacuum pressure of 10^-10 Torr, cavity finesse of ~208, and a supermode waist of ~1.7-9.5 micrometers.
main_assumptions: The effective response of the cavity length to dispersive shifts is assumed to be subject to the finite bandwidth of the PDH servo.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the vacuum assembly and cavity/sample stack; Figure 2 details the 4-axis nanopositioner; Figure 3 displays cavity transmission spectra; Figure 4 shows the laser stabilization chain; Figure 5 presents in-situ sample images; Figure 7 shows the power spectral density of cavity length noise.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the scientific motivation, describes the construction and components of the cryogenic/UHV apparatus, details the optical and electrical measurement methods, presents performance metrics for stability and positioning, and concludes with the demonstration of the integrated platform.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to cavity QED and quantum optics as it demonstrates a method to achieve high-cooperativity regimes using multimode supermodes, providing a platform for studying driven-dissipative phase transitions and light-matter interactions in 2D materials.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Overview of the apparatus. (a) Top view of the full vacuum assembly. The sample exchange port is a 10" conflat window that is removed during exchange. (b) Cross-sectional view of the assembly, sliced along line I in panel (a), which is the centerline of the cavity mount and sample stack. Important components are labeled. (c) Cross-sectional view of the assembly, sliced along line II, the centerline of the cryostat. The sample stage has been removed for clarity, though the copper cold finger braid is shown. (Inset) Magnified view of the cavity and coupling lenses.

FIG. 2. Cavity mount and sample positioning stage. (a) Schematic of the cavity mount, showing in purple (from top to bottom) the coupling lens, cavity mirrors, and collimat- ing lens. In cyan are the sample stack attocubes, the cavity attocube, and the fast piezo. (b) Photograph of the cav- ity mount, before installation into the vacuum chamber. (c) Photograph of the cavity in the chamber with a 2D sample positioned at the cavity mid-plane.

FIG. 3. Cavity spectrum and linewidth. (a) Transmission spectra of near-confocal (blue curve) and confocal (orange curve) optical cavity geometries. The near-confocal curve is scaled vertically by 2× and offset for clarity. (b) High-resolution scan over the TEM00 transmission peak taken away from confocality at L/R = 1.125, exhibiting a linewidth of 64(1) MHz.

FIG. 4. Schematic of the laser and cavity stabilization scheme. The arrows point toward the device to which the element is being locked. 1) The diode laser (DL) is stabilized to a Rb vapor cell via a modulation transfer spectroscopy lock. 2) The science cavity length is locked to the DL via a Pound-Drever-Hall (PDH) lock, with the transmitted signal detected on a photodiode (PD). 3) The transfer cavity is locked to the DL via PDH. 4) The two titanium-sapphire lasers (Ti:Saphs) are independently stabilized to the transfer cavity via PDH locks at any chosen wavelengths. The Ti:Saphs serve as the longitudinal and transverse pump beams for the science cavity and sample, respectively.

FIG. 5. In situ optical imaging of TMD sample. (a) Optical micrograph of the sample taken outside the vacuum chamber under 20× magnification. Red circles indicate features away from the sample used to align the sample to the cavity supermode. The reader will notice the many errant flakes and fabrication residue on the sapphire substrate. That these do not affect the cavity finesse is because the supermode forms at the transverse location where we will want to pump the cavity, which is at the sample (hBN-encapsulated monolayer WSe2) indicated by the white arrow. (b) Image of the sample taken through the confocal cavity emission when a broadband LED source is resonantly coupled to cavity...
